title: Substack's anti-slop plan points to the next phase of the AI boom
excerpt: When AI is everywhere, offering AI-free options may be very appealing. That's the bet Substack is making.
content_html:
By Peter Kafka

You're currently following this author! Want to unfollow? Unsubscribe via the link in your email.

Chief Correspondent covering media and technology

The global economy is increasingly hinging on the notion that AI will be inexorably intertwined with every part of our lives.

Also increasing: signs that consumers — or at least some consumers — don't feel great about that, at all.

You can see that most clearly in the anti-data center backlash, which is picking up steam. Or when fans of A24, the hipster-ish film studio, heap scorn on the indie for daring to work with Google's DeepMind AI lab.

But I think it's just as telling when people you wouldn't think would be worried about AI say they're worried about AI. Or, at least, that they understand why you're worried about AI.

That's the gist of Tuesday's announcement from Substack, the newsletter tool/aspiring social network: It says that AI slop is overwhelming the internet, and it wants to fight back.

For now, that effort amounts to giving Substack users access to a tool created by Pangram, an AI-detection startup. Down the line, the company says, it may introduce other features, like the ability for Substack readers to keep AI-generated content out of their feeds.

Substack CEO Chris Best takes pains to say Substack isn't opposed to the use of AI in general or in the creation of Substack content:

We're not against people using AI to assist their work, and we think people should be free to choose which tools they use to express themselves. Some on the platform are human-writing maximalists, others are publicly exploring the frontier of using AI tools while sweating the details to make work they stand behind. We use AI all the time at Substack to write software, do research, and build product features like clipping, translations, and more.

But common sense tells you that Substack wouldn't be putting together an anti-AI plan if it wasn't worried about AI. Or if it didn't think its users were worried about AI.

The giveaway is when Best uses the dreaded "L"-word to describe what might happen if AI growth isn't curtailed: "One thing we do know is that we don't want to wait until your Substack app turns into LinkedIn before we start to learn and make progress."

LinkedIn! How gross — at least in Best's mind, or at least in the minds of the people Best imagines use Substack.

Except that 1) I've seen plenty of crummy Substack screeds, and sometimes I see a really insightful LinkedIn post. It's a wide world of content out there. And 2) a couple months ago, LinkedIn said it was trying to fight AI slop, too.

I think there's a pretty good debate to be had about much of this. Are people opposed to any use of AI in writing or any other kind of communication? How much is too much? Who decides how much of it I get to see? And honestly: If the SVP of widget production wants to use ChatGPT to write a boring post about widget workflow automation, who really cares?

But I don't think there's any debate that lots of real people have real anxiety about AI.

Tech leaders act befuddled about this. They shouldn't.

They spent a couple of years telling us that AI was going to reorder the entire world — and very likely most people's jobs — and that its progress was unstoppable. During that same time, many of them explicitly linked themselves to Donald Trump — a man who many, many people do not like. It would be weird if people weren't freaked out about this.

I do agree with the tech POV that AI is unstoppable. Which is why I think all of this most likely nets out with a labeling regime.

In that world, the average person will spend a lot of time encountering AI, and probably won't know or think much about it. And for some consumers, and some brands, an "AI-free" label will be a selling point, the same way a certain slice of our population pays a premium for organic food.

And for a bunch of us, it may be something we care about in differing amounts, in different times and contexts. I truly love an organic hot dog — and I also eat and buy a lot of super-gnarly junk food made who-knows-where using who-knows-what. You may be fine with AI in your LinkedIn, but hate it in your Spotify.

The tech industry may be right that AI is going to be inevitable. But "inevitable" is different from "accepted." And there will be money and opportunity in giving people places where it isn't.

Read next

Peter Kafka

You're currently following this author! Want to unfollow? Unsubscribe via the link in your email.

Peter covers media and technology for Business Insider; previously he has worked at Vox, Recode, AllThingsD, and Forbes. He was also the first hire at Silicon Alley Insider, Business Insider's predecessor.

  • AI
  • LinkedIn

blocks.html_en: For now, that effort amounts to giving Substack users access to a tool created by Pangram, an AI-detection startup. Down the line, the company says, it may introduce other features, like the ability for Substack readers to keep AI-generated content out of their feeds.

sentences.en: Down the line, the company says, it may introduce other features, like the ability for Substack readers to keep AI-generated content out of their feeds.
sentences.zh: 该公司表示，它可能会引入其他功能，例如 Substack 读者能够将人工智能生成的内容排除在其提要之外。

blocks.html_en: Substack CEO Chris Best takes pains to say Substack isn't opposed to the use of AI in general or in the creation of Substack content:

sentences.en: Substack CEO Chris Best takes pains to say Substack isn't opposed to the use of AI in general or in the creation of Substack content:
sentences.zh: Substack 首席执行官 Chris Best 煞费苦心地说 Substack 并不反对在一般情况下使用人工智能或在 Substack 内容的创建中使用人工智能：

blocks.html_en: We're not against people using AI to assist their work, and we think people should be free to choose which tools they use to express themselves. Some on the platform are human-writing maximalists, others are publicly exploring the frontier of using AI tools while sweating the details to make work they stand behind. We use AI all the time at Substack to write software, do research, and build product features like clipping, translations, and more.

sentences.en: We're not against people using AI to assist their work, and we think people should be free to choose which tools they use to express themselves.
sentences.zh: 我们并不反对人们使用人工智能来协助他们的工作，我们认为人们应该自由选择使用哪些工具来表达自己。

sentences.en: Some on the platform are human-writing maximalists, others are publicly exploring the frontier of using AI tools while sweating the details to make work they stand behind.
sentences.zh: 该平台上的一些人是人类写作的最大化主义者，另一些人则公开探索使用人工智能工具的前沿，同时致力于细节以完成他们所支持的工作。

sentences.en: We use AI all the time at Substack to write software, do research, and build product features like clipping, translations, and more.
sentences.zh: 我们在 Substack 一直使用人工智能来编写软件、进行研究并构建剪辑、翻译等产品功能。

blocks.html_en: But common sense tells you that Substack wouldn't be putting together an anti-AI plan if it wasn't worried about AI. Or if it didn't think its users were worried about AI.

sentences.en: But common sense tells you that Substack wouldn't be putting together an anti-AI plan if it wasn't worried about AI.

sentences.en: Or if it didn't think its users were worried about AI.
sentences.zh: 或者如果它不认为用户担心人工智能。

blocks.html_en: The giveaway is when Best uses the dreaded "L"-word to describe what might happen if AI growth isn't curtailed: "One thing we do know is that we don't want to wait until your Substack app turns into LinkedIn before we start to learn and make progress."

sentences.en: The giveaway is when Best uses the dreaded "L"-word to describe what might happen if AI growth isn't curtailed: "One thing we do know is that we don't want to wait until your Substack app turns into LinkedIn before we start to learn and make progress."

blocks.html_en: LinkedIn! How gross — at least in Best's mind, or at least in the minds of the people Best imagines use Substack.

sentences.zh: 领英！

sentences.zh: 多么恶心——至少在 Best 看来，或者至少在 Best 想象使用 Substack 的人看来。

blocks.html_en: Except that 1) I've seen plenty of crummy Substack screeds, and sometimes I see a really insightful LinkedIn post. It's a wide world of content out there. And 2) a couple months ago, LinkedIn said it was trying to fight AI slop, too.

sentences.zh: 这是一个广阔的内容世界。

sentences.en: And 2) a couple months ago, LinkedIn said it was trying to fight AI slop, too.
sentences.zh: 2) 几个月前，LinkedIn 表示它也在努力打击人工智能滥用现象。

blocks.html_en: I think there's a pretty good debate to be had about much of this. Are people opposed to any use of AI in writing or any other kind of communication? How much is too much? Who decides how much of it I get to see? And honestly: If the SVP of widget production wants to use ChatGPT to write a boring post about widget workflow automation, who really cares?

sentences.en: I think there's a pretty good debate to be had about much of this.

sentences.en: How much is too much?
sentences.zh: 多少算太多？

sentences.en: Who decides how much of it I get to see?
sentences.zh: 谁决定我能看到多少内容？

sentences.en: And honestly: If the SVP of widget production wants to use ChatGPT to write a boring post about widget workflow automation, who really cares?
sentences.zh: 老实说：如果小部件生产的高级副总裁想要使用 ChatGPT 写一篇关于小部件工作流程自动化的无聊文章，谁真的在乎呢？

blocks.html_en: But I don't think there's any debate that lots of real people have real anxiety about AI.

sentences.en: But I don't think there's any debate that lots of real people have real anxiety about AI.
sentences.zh: 但我不认为有任何争论表明许多真实的人对人工智能有真正的焦虑。

blocks.html_en: Tech leaders act befuddled about this. They shouldn't.

sentences.en: It would be weird if people weren't freaked out about this.

blocks.html_en: In that world, the average person will spend a lot of time encountering AI, and probably won't know or think much about it. sentences.en: And for some consumers, and some brands, an "AI-free" label will be a selling point, the same way a certain slice of our population pays a premium for organic food.

sentences.zh: 我真的很喜欢有机热狗——而且我还吃和买了很多超级粗糙的垃圾食品，这些垃圾食品是用谁知道的东西在哪里制作的。

sentences.en: You may be fine with AI in your LinkedIn, but hate it in your Spotify.
sentences.zh: 您可能对 LinkedIn 中的人工智能感到满意，但讨厌 Spotify 中的人工智能。

blocks.html_en: The tech industry may be right that AI is going to be inevitable. But "inevitable" is different from "accepted." And there will be money and opportunity in giving people places where it isn't.

sentences.en: But "inevitable" is different from "accepted."

sentences.en: And there will be money and opportunity in giving people places where it isn't.

content_html_zh:
作者：Peter Kafka 您目前正在关注该作者！ 想要取消关注吗？ 通过电子邮件中的链接取消订阅。 媒体和科技首席记者

全球经济越来越依赖于这样一种观念：人工智能将不可避免地与我们生活的方方面面交织在一起。

越来越多的迹象表明，消费者——或者至少是一些消费者——对此根本感觉不太好。

你可以在反数据中心的强烈抵制中看到这一点，这种抵制正在升温。 或者，当时尚电影工作室 A24 的粉丝们对这个敢于与谷歌 DeepMind 人工智能实验室合作的独立电影人嗤之以鼻时。

但我认为，当你认为不会担心人工智能的人说他们担心人工智能时，这也同样能说明问题。 或者，至少，他们理解你为什么担心人工智能。

这是新闻通讯工具/雄心勃勃的社交网络 Substack 周二发布的公告的要点：它表示人工智能的颓势正在压倒互联网，它想要反击。

目前，这项工作相当于让 Substack 用户可以访问 AI 检测初创公司 Pangram 创建的工具。 该公司表示，它可能会引入其他功能，例如 Substack 读者能够将人工智能生成的内容排除在其提要之外。

Substack 首席执行官 Chris Best 煞费苦心地说 Substack 并不反对在一般情况下使用人工智能或在 Substack 内容的创建中使用人工智能：

我们并不反对人们使用人工智能来协助他们的工作，我们认为人们应该自由选择使用哪些工具来表达自己。 该平台上的一些人是人类写作的最大化主义者，另一些人则公开探索使用人工智能工具的前沿，同时致力于细节以完成他们所支持的工作。 我们在 Substack 一直使用人工智能来编写软件、进行研究并构建剪辑、翻译等产品功能。

但常识告诉你，如果 Substack 不担心人工智能，它就不会制定反人工智能计划。 或者如果它不认为用户担心人工智能。

当 Best 使用可怕的“L”字来描述如果人工智能增长不受到限制时可能发生的情况时，就暴露了这一点：“我们确实知道的一件事是，我们不想等到你的 Substack 应用程序变成 LinkedIn 才开始学习和取得进展。”

领英！ 多么恶心——至少在 Best 看来，或者至少在 Best 想象使用 Substack 的人看来。

除此之外，1）我看过很多糟糕的 Substack 文章，有时我会看到一篇非常有洞察力的 LinkedIn 帖子。 这是一个广阔的内容世界。 2) 几个月前，LinkedIn 表示它也在努力打击人工智能滥用现象。

我认为关于这方面的大部分内容都值得进行一场很好的辩论。 人们是否反对在书面或任何其他形式的交流中使用人工智能？ 多少算太多？ 谁决定我能看到多少内容？ 老实说：如果小部件生产的高级副总裁想要使用 ChatGPT 写一篇关于小部件工作流程自动化的无聊文章，谁真的在乎呢？

但我不认为有任何争论表明许多真实的人对人工智能有真正的焦虑。

科技领导者对此表现出困惑。 他们不应该。

他们花了几年时间告诉我们，人工智能将重新安排整个世界——很可能还会改变大多数人的工作——而且它的进步是不可阻挡的。 与此同时，他们中的许多人明确地将自己与唐纳德·特朗普联系起来——一个很多很多人都不喜欢的人。 如果人们不对此感到害怕，那才奇怪。

我确实同意科技观点，即人工智能是不可阻挡的。 这就是为什么我认为所有这一切很可能通过标签制度来解决。

在那个世界里，普通人会花很多时间接触人工智能，并且可能不会了解或思考太多。 对于一些消费者和一些品牌来说，“无人工智能”标签将是一个卖点，就像我们中的一部分人为有机食品支付溢价一样。

对于我们很多人来说，在不同的时间和背景下，我们可能会以不同的程度关心它。 我真的很喜欢有机热狗——而且我还吃和买了很多超级粗糙的垃圾食品，这些垃圾食品是用谁知道的东西在哪里制作的。 您可能对 LinkedIn 中的人工智能感到满意，但讨厌 Spotify 中的人工智能。

科技行业认为人工智能将不可避免，这或许是正确的。 但“不可避免”与“接受”不同。 为人们提供原本没有的地方也会有金钱和机会。
